questions.text: The Vijayanagara Empire was founded in which year?
questions.explanation: The Vijayanagara Empire was founded in 1336 CE by brothers Harihara I and Bukka I of the Sangama dynasty, with the sage Vidyaranya's guidance. The capital was Vijayanagara (modern Hampi, Karnataka) on the banks of the Tungabhadra River.

questions.text: The Battle of Talikota (1565 CE), which destroyed the Vijayanagara Empire, was fought against a coalition of which kingdoms?
options: Bijapur, Bidar, Ahmadnagar, and Golconda Sultanates
questions.explanation: The Battle of Talikota (January 23, 1565) was fought between Vijayanagara (under Aliya Rama Raya) and a coalition of four Deccan Sultanates: Bijapur (Adil Shahi), Bidar (Barid Shahi), Ahmadnagar (Nizam Shahi), and Golconda (Qutb Shahi). Berar was NOT in the coalition. Vijayanagara was decisively defeated.

questions.text: Which was the greatest ruler of the Vijayanagara Empire who wrote 'Amuktamalyada' in Telugu?
questions.explanation: Krishnadeva Raya (1509–1529 CE, Tuluva dynasty) was the greatest Vijayanagara ruler. He wrote Amuktamalyada in Telugu — considered one of the finest Telugu literary works. He is called 'Telugu rājula rāju' (king of Telugu kings) and his court had the Ashta Diggajas (eight Telugu poets).

questions.text: The Bahmani Sultanate was founded in 1347 CE by:
questions.explanation: Hasan Gangu (who took the name Alauddin Bahman Shah) founded the Bahmani Sultanate in 1347 CE after rebelling against Muhammad bin Tughlaq's Delhi Sultanate. He was a general who refused to follow orders after the forced capital transfer disaster.

questions.text: The Vittala Temple at Hampi is famous for a 'Stone Chariot' that now appears on which Indian currency note?
questions.explanation: The stone chariot (ratha) at the Vittala Temple in Hampi is depicted on the ₹50 note. It is a freestanding stone structure built during the Vijayanagara period (likely Krishnadeva Raya's era), shaped like a temple chariot. The Vittala Temple also has 56 'musical pillars' that produce different sounds.

questions.text: The Gol Gumbaz at Bijapur is the tomb of which Adil Shahi ruler?
questions.explanation: Gol Gumbaz (completed 1656) is the tomb of Muhammad Adil Shah of the Bijapur Sultanate. Its dome has a diameter of ~44 metres — one of the largest in the world. The 'Whispering Gallery' inside the drum of the dome transmits sound across the diameter. Architect: Yaqut of Dabhol.

questions.text: Mahmud Gawan, the brilliant Bahmani minister, was executed on the basis of:
options: A genuine conspiracy against the Sultan he organised
A forged letter shown to Sultan Shamsuddin with his seal
His attempt to convert to Hinduism
questions.explanation: Mahmud Gawan was falsely accused of treason — his enemies forged a letter to Vijayanagara bearing his official seal and showed it to Sultan Shamsuddin Gawan (Muhammad III), who ordered his execution in a rage. The Sultan reportedly regretted this immediately but the damage was done. Mahmud Gawan's death in 1481 accelerated the Bahmani Sultanate's disintegration.

questions.text: Hyderabad city was founded in 1591 CE by which ruler of the Golconda Sultanate?
questions.explanation: Muhammad Quli Qutb Shah (1580–1611 CE) founded the city of Hyderabad in 1591 CE, building it on the south bank of the Musi River across from Golconda Fort. He also built the Charminar (1591) — the iconic four-minaret structure that has become the symbol of Hyderabad.

questions.text: The Persian ambassador Abdur Razzaq who left the most famous account of Vijayanagara visited during the reign of:
questions.explanation: Abdur Razzaq visited Vijayanagara in 1443 CE during the reign of Devaraya II (Sangama dynasty). He wrote: 'The city of Vijayanagara is such that the pupil of the eye has never seen a place like it.' This is the most famous and detailed foreign account of Vijayanagara. Krishnadeva Raya was visited by Portuguese travellers Domingo Paes and Fernão Nunes.

questions.text: The 'Ashta Diggajas' at Krishnadeva Raya's court refers to:
options: Eight generals who guarded the eight directions
Eight Telugu poets who were his court luminaries
Eight ministers of his Ashtapradhan council
Eight Jain scholars who debated in his court
questions.explanation: Ashta Diggajas ('Eight Directional Elephants') = the eight Telugu literary giants at Krishnadeva Raya's court. The greatest was Allasani Peddana (author of Manuchritra, called 'Andhra Kavita Pitamaha'). Others include Nandi Thimmana and Tenali Ramakrishna (Tenali Raman — the clever court jester). SSC occasionally asks about Peddana's epithet.

questions.text: What was the capital of the Bahmani Sultanate after it was moved from Gulbarga?
questions.explanation: The Bahmani capital was moved from Gulbarga (Ahsanabad) to Bidar by Sultan Ahmad Shah I (Ahmad Shah Wali) in 1424 CE. Bidar remained the capital until the Sultanate's dissolution. The Madrasa of Mahmud Gawan, one of the finest Islamic buildings in India, is located in Bidar.

questions.text: Which of the five Deccan Sultanates was famous for its diamond mines and was the origin of the Kohinoor diamond?
questions.explanation: The Golconda Sultanate (Qutb Shahi) controlled the diamond mines of the Kollur mine (near Guntur, Andhra Pradesh) — which produced many famous diamonds including the Kohinoor, the Hope Diamond, and the Regent Diamond. 'Golconda diamond' became synonymous with great wealth.

questions.text: Vijayanagara was founded with the guidance of which saint of the Sringeri Matha?
questions.explanation: Vidyaranya (also known as Madhava, the philosopher) of the Sringeri Matha guided and inspired Harihara I and Bukka I in founding the Vijayanagara Empire and returning to Hinduism after their period as Tughlaq officers. Vidyaranya is considered the spiritual father of the Vijayanagara Empire.

questions.text: Which dynasty of the Vijayanagara Empire produced Krishnadeva Raya?
questions.explanation: Krishnadeva Raya belonged to the Tuluva dynasty — the third of four dynasties that ruled Vijayanagara. The Tuluva dynasty produced the empire's greatest rulers. The Sangama dynasty was the founding dynasty (Harihara, Bukka, Devaraya). After Talikota, the Aravidu dynasty continued as a rump state.

questions.text: The Charminar, the iconic monument of Hyderabad, was built in:
questions.explanation: The Charminar (Four Minarets) was built in 1591 CE by Muhammad Quli Qutb Shah — the same year he founded Hyderabad. It was built to commemorate the end of a plague epidemic. Each of the four minarets is 56 metres tall. It is now a UNESCO-tentative listed site and the symbol of Hyderabad.

questions.text: The Bahmani Sultanate disintegrated into five successor states between approximately:
questions.explanation: The Bahmani Sultanate gradually disintegrated as its provinces declared independence between ~1490–1518 CE — forming the five Deccan Sultanates: Ahmadnagar, Bijapur, Bidar, Golconda, and Berar. The last Bahmani Sultan Kalimullah was a powerless figurehead; the Sultanate formally ended in 1527 CE.
